knowledge.summary: Last In, First Out (LIFO) is a principle used in data structures to manage the ordering of elements. It prioritizes the most recent addition as the first one to be removed.
key_points: Elements are added and removed from only one end of the structure.
This principle is often used in stacks, queues, and certain types of algorithms.
In a stack, the last element pushed becomes the first one popped.
knowledge.common_confusion: It's important to remember that LIFO means the most recent addition comes out first, not the oldest as in FIFO (First In, First Out) structures like queues.

knowledge.summary: Adding an element onto the top of a stack. The size of the stack increases by one after a push operation.
key_points: It adds an element to the top of the stack.
The top of the stack is where the most recently added element resides.
knowledge.common_confusion: A common mistake is assuming that pushing an element to an empty stack creates a bottom element instead of a top one.

knowledge.summary: The Pop operation removes the topmost element from a stack.
key_points: It reduces the size of the stack by one.
The removed element is usually returned as the result of the operation.
If the stack is empty, attempting to pop will cause an error.
knowledge.common_confusion: Some beginners might mistakenly think that Pop operation affects elements other than the topmost.

knowledge.summary: The Peek operation allows you to view the top element of a stack without removing it.
key_points: It's a read-only operation that gives information about the next item to be processed.
This is useful when you want to check the stack's top element before performing any actions.
However, it does not alter the stack.
knowledge.common_confusion: One common beginner mistake is to assume that peeking and popping are the same operations. Peek only looks at the top of the stack, while pop removes it.

knowledge.summary: A stack overflow occurs when a recursive function or method call exceeds the maximum depth allowed, resulting in an error.
key_points: Recursive functions or methods should have a base case to prevent infinite loops and stack overflows
Exceeding the maximum function call depth leads to a 'Maximum recursion depth exceeded' error
Avoid using excessive nested function calls to avoid potential stack overflow
knowledge.common_confusion: Confusing recursive functions with iterative ones, leading to potential stack overflows

knowledge.summary: A singly linked list is a linear data structure consisting of nodes, where each node contains a data item and a reference (link or next pointer) to the next node in the sequence.
key_points: Each node in a singly linked list has two parts: data and a link to the next node.
The last node of the list has no next node (it is NULL).
Insertion and deletion operations can be performed at any position.
knowledge.common_confusion: A common beginner mistake is forgetting to update the 'next' pointer in a newly added node when inserting at a certain position.

knowledge.summary: Last In, First Out (LIFO) is a principle that refers to the order in which elements are added and removed from a stack. Elements added last will be the first ones to be removed.
key_points: Stack structure follows the LIFO principle, meaning the last element added is the first one to be removed.
Pushing and popping operations take place only at the top of the stack.
A stack can have a bounded capacity, leading to a potential 'stack overflow' state.
knowledge.common_confusion: Beginners may confuse stacks with queues, which follow the First In, First Out (FIFO) principle.

knowledge.summary: The Push operation in a stack adds an element to the collection. It operates on the top of the stack, placing the new element at the top, making it the most recently added item.
key_points: Adds an element to the stack
Places the new element at the top
Makes the added item the most recent
knowledge.common_confusion: Remember that a stack follows LIFO (Last In, First Out) order, so the last item pushed is the first one popped.

knowledge.summary: Removes the most recently added element from a stack. If the stack is not empty, pop operation returns the topmost element.
key_points: 1. Pop removes the last item added to the stack.
2. It makes the second-last item the new top.
3. If the stack is empty, an error occurs as nothing can be removed.
knowledge.common_confusion: Beginners might confuse pop with peek operation which returns the top element without removing it.

knowledge.summary: The Peek operation in a stack returns the top element without removing it.
key_points: It allows to view the most recently added element without modifying the stack.
This operation is useful for checking the top item before performing a pop operation.
knowledge.common_confusion: One common beginner mistake is to assume that peek automatically removes the top element, which it does not.

knowledge.summary: The Operationskeller is a concept of a stack in computer science, first proposed by Klaus Samelson and Friedrich L. Bauer in 1955 at Technical University Munich. It served as the basis for the implementation of a two-level stack in various computing machines.
key_points: Proposed in 1955
First described by Samelson and Bauer
Formed the basis for a two-level stack
knowledge.common_confusion: It's important to remember that Operationskeller is the original name of the stack concept, not a specific type or implementation of the data structure.

knowledge.summary: Stack is an abstract data type that functions like a collection where elements are added (Push) and removed (Pop) following the Last In, First Out (LIFO) principle. It's named after a physical stack of items.
key_points: Elements are added and removed from only one end (top).
Stack may be implemented as a singly linked list or array.
If the stack reaches its maximum capacity, it cannot accept more elements until some are removed.
knowledge.common_confusion: Mistakenly assuming stack is First In, First Out (FIFO) rather than Last In, First Out (LIFO).

knowledge.summary: Underflow occurs when a stack operation is performed on an empty stack.
key_points: Upon execution of 'stack top' or 'pop' operations, underflow leads to undefined behavior.
Checking if the stack is empty before performing operations helps prevent this issue.
An empty stack can be detected by checking if the top variable (indicating the number of items pushed) equals zero.
knowledge.common_confusion: Neglecting to check for an empty stack before executing 'stack top' or 'pop' operations can cause unexpected errors.

knowledge.summary: A stack can be implemented using an array, where the top of the stack corresponds to the last index of the array.
key_points: The push operation adds an element to the end of the array.
The pop operation removes and discards the last element from the array.
The peek operation returns the value at the last index without removing it.
knowledge.common_confusion: It's important to keep track of the number of elements in the stack as arrays have a fixed size, so if the stack overflows or underflows, an error occurs.

knowledge.summary: A Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) principle is a sequence where the last element added to a data structure (like a stack) is the first one to be removed.
key_points: In a LIFO structure, elements are added and removed from only one end called the top.
The order of elements in a LIFO structure follows the principle: last in, first out.
LIFO is useful for tasks such as undo functions in software programs.
knowledge.common_confusion: It's important to remember that once an element is popped from a stack, it can't be retrieved again until more recent elements are removed.
